title: Paper Moebius Bands
speaker: Richard Schwartz
affiliation: Brown University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: You can make a paper Moebius band by starting with a 1 by L rectangle, giving it a twist, and then gluing the ends together. The question is: How short can you make L and still succeed in making the thing? This question goes back to B. Halpern and C. Weaver in 1977. The previously known lower bound was π/2 and the previously known upper bound, conjectured to be the true answer, is 3‾√.

proof_stages.label: Paper Möbius bands and the Halpern-Weaver problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines a paper Möbius band as a smooth locally intrinsically isometric realization of a rectangular Möbius strip, states the aspect-ratio minimization problem for embeddings, and reviews benchmark constructions and conjectures.

proof_stages.label: Alternating-sum sphere-curve lower bound
proof_stages.summary: The Halpern-Weaver lower bound is sketched by associating to the band an alternating-sum polygonal curve outside the unit sphere, tangent to its natural light-cone structure, whose length at least pi gives aspect ratio at least pi/2.

proof_stages.label: Existence of a T-pattern in short bands
proof_stages.summary: Using the circle of bend images and a homological involution argument on perpendicular pairs, the speaker explains why a sufficiently short embedded paper Möbius band must contain two perpendicular coplanar bend images, called a T-pattern.

proof_stages.label: Metric optimization after cutting along a T-pattern
proof_stages.summary: After cutting the band along a T-pattern, convex-hull and slope constraints first give a golden-ratio lower bound and then a coupled constrained optimization gives the speaker's improved explicit lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Finite tensegrity reduction of the root-three conjectures
proof_stages.summary: The second theorem models selected bend lines and transverse edges as a finite tensegrity and reduces both root-three conjectures to positivity of three explicit semialgebraic functions in fourteen variables.

proof_stages.label: Embeddedness topology forcing stretched configurations
proof_stages.summary: Because immersed low-ratio T-patterns exist, embeddedness is used through crossing diagrams and endpoint-domain capacity estimates to force one of finitely many stretched tensegrity configurations.

proof_stages.label: Numerical evidence and prospective certification
proof_stages.summary: The remaining inequalities are tested numerically by random and hill-climbing searches, and the discussion explains what local analysis, semialgebraic decision procedures, or polynomial-positivity methods would be needed to turn the reduction into a rigorous proof.

examples.name: Accordion-folded square cheat
examples.note: The accordion-folded square would make the aspect ratio effectively zero if arbitrary creasing were allowed, illustrating why the admissible maps must be smoothly isometrically approximable.
